publicationstmt.publisher: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2023-09-20 00:15:35 UTC.
profiledesc.langusage: Description is written in: Undetermined, Code for undetermined script script.

repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.abstract: Poetry, plays, short stories, lectures, and journals written by Professor Martin W. Sampson. Also includes correspondence and ephemera.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Martin W. Sampson papers (1870-1969, bulk 1887-1930) predominantly contains poetic, dramatic, and other fictional works by Professor Sampson, lecture notes taken by Sampson while Professor of English at Indiana University, and personal journals kept from 1915 to 1922. Includes manuscripts and handwritten drafts of The Good Giant and The Soul of a Professor. Collection contains a small amount of personal correspondence, Cornell ephemera, and materials originating from A.D. White, John F. Lyman and the Lyman family, and the Iota Chapter of the Kappa Alpha Theta fraternity.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Martin Wright Sampson was born on September 7, 1866, in Cincinnati, Ohio. He was the son of William S. Sampson and Virginia Ada (Wright) Sampson. He studied at the University of Cincinnati, earning his A.B. in 1888 and A.M. in 1890. He did graduate work in Germany, France, England, and Ireland, including study at the University of Munich in 1888. From 1889 to 1891, he was first instructor and then assistant professor of English at the University of Iowa. During the following year (1892), he taught at Stanford. For the next thirteen years, he was professor of English at Indiana University (1893-1906). Professor Sampson came to Cornell University in 1908 as acting professor of English, and soon became one of the most prominent members of the faculty. He became head of the English Department at Cornell in 1909 and was named Goldwin Smith Professor of English Literature in 1912 as well as an honorary member of the Class of 1912. He was a founder of the Manuscript Club, made up of young writers, and hosted the group at his home each Saturday evening during the school year. In 1919, he founded the Sampson Fine Arts Prize, given annually to a student who showed the most intelligent appreciation of the graphic and plastic arts and of photography. The award is still given annually to members of the senior class who have consistently demonstrated academic excellent, commitment, and achievement, particularly in the field of the history of art.

Professor Sampson edited a number of works in the field of English literature, notably the poems of Milton. He was well known for his original writings, including stories for children, plays, satires, and poetry. Professor Sampson was a joint author with E.O. Holland of a textbook entitled Written and Oral Composition (1907). He was a translator of Hans Wolfgang Singer's Dresden 1: The Royal Picture Gallery (1907), an editor of numerous works, inlcuding The Lyric and Dramatic Poems of John Milton (1901), John Webster's The White Devil and the Duchess of Malfy (1904), Elizabeth Gaskell's Cranford (1905), Selections from Irving's Sketch-book (1907), Shakespeare's Two Gentlemen of Verona (1912), Thomas Middleton (1915), Selections from John Milton (1925), and Henry James' Daisy Miller (1927) and The Ambassadors (1930). The Good Giant, a series of stories for children, was published in 1928 from stories he wrote for his own four children. His attitude towards academic problems was entertainingly presented in a one-act satire, The Soul of a Professor (published in Cornell University Plays, 1932). A volume of his poems, Voices in the Forest, was published posthumously in 1933. He was frequently asked to read from his own writings and readings he held outside of Sibley Hall for engineering students eventually became a part of the curriculum for the Sibley College of Engineering. He was also a contributor to literary journals, a member of Phi Beta Kappa, Phi Kappa Sigma, and Quill and Dagger. He was a trustee of Wells College. In January of 1930, he began writing a column, "The Swinging Bridge," for the Cornell Alumni News.

During World War I, Sampson was instrumental in establishing and raising funds for the Cornell unit of the American Ambulance Field Service. This group had the distinction of being the first to get to the front under the American flag. He edited Camion Letters from American College Man: Volunteer Divers of the American Field Service in France (1917) and just before his death, he completed editing Military Records of Cornell University in the World War (1930).

In 1892, Sampson married Frances Van Rensselaer Gardiner, who died in 1909. In 1910, he married his second wife Julia Dauchy Pattison of Troy, New York. They had four children together, Stephen Hastings, Martin Wright, Jr., Margaret Virginia, and Edward Coolidge. His son Martin Wright Sampson, Jr., would also go on to become a professor at Cornell University.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Martin W. Sampson papers, 14-12-116. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Cornell University Libraries has editions of the following works by Sampson:
Sampson, M.W. & Ernest O. Holland. (1907). Written and Oral Compostion. New York: American Book Company. Call # arV14659
Sampson, M.W. (n.d.) The Soul of a Professor. Ithaca. Call # PS3537.A5 S7
Sampson, M.W. (1928). The Good Giant. Boston: Houghton Mifflin. Call # Rare Books PZ8.S19 G6 and # Rare Books PZ8.S19 G6
Sampson, M.W. (1933). Voices of the Forest. Ithaca: The Cayuga Press. Call # Rare Books PS3537.A51 V8

separatedmaterial.head: Separated Materials
separatedmaterial.p: Drummond, A.M. (Ed.) (1932). Cornell University Plays. New York: S. French Ltd.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sampson's high school diploma from the Union Board of High Schols of Cincinnati, Ohio.

did.unittitle: Bachelor of Arts diploma, University of Cincinnati
Master of Arts diploma, University of Cincinnati

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes letters written to his grandmother while studying abroad in Munich, Germany, correspondence from Buckingham Palace regarding the Prince of Wales' Nationa Relief Fund, 1915, a letter from J.G. Schurman on the event of his resignation as Cornell President, 1920, and letters written to his children, 1930.

did.unittitle: Publication contract for The Introduction, Notes, and Glossary of Four Plays of Middleton for Felix E. Schelling's Masterpieces of the English Drama

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Publication and copyright agreement between Sampson and the American Book Company.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a photo of Max Elser, 1909, photocopied sketches of memorials for George Fisher Baker and Frank Barton, "Resolutions of the Trustees and Faculty of Cornell Univeristy on the Passing of Professor Martin W. Sampson, alumni fundraising booklets, and a Class of 1917 Freshman Banquet menu with Sampson's signature in the cover.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes materials regarding John F. Lyman and the Lyman family, letters from A.D. White, early photos of Cornell campus, class of 1937 Freshman Banquet booklet, class of 1909 and 1939 reunion photos, and a booklet from the opening of the Cornell Club of New York building.

did.unittitle: Etching of A.D. White by Jacques Reich

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Artist's proof from the copper, no. 25 of 50 copies. Signed by Reich and A.D. White. Includes photo of Bailey Hall, undated.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes addresses, fictionalized stories and poems about memebers and meetings of the Manuscript Club in Hell.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A certificate of indebtedness to the Iota Chapter of Kappa Alpha Theta and final reports of the chapter before its 1987 dissolution.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Bookplates, a playbill for Sampson's comedy, "A Photographic Exposure" (1902), a passenger list for the R.M.S Saxonia, 1924, two small possibly passport photos of unidentified individuals, and a copy of the Bulletin des Francais, assumed 1915. Includes several newspaper clippings of articles written by Sampson or regarding Sampson's passing.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dramas, comedies, and satires. Includes manuscripts of "Gamaliel, the High Priest," "The Soul of a Professor," and a short play titled "A Midnight Dialogue between the Library and Willard Straight Hall."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Drafts and finished poetry written by Sampson. Includes a leatherbound poem entitled "Prologue," written for a production of Shakespeare around the 300th anniversary of his death.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Musings, essays, short stories, and excerpts of longer fiction works written by Sampson.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Undated short plays written by Sampson in the style of Chekhov, Ibsen, Wilde, Maeterlinck, and other dramatists, and one based on elements from many melodramas.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a dedication to Sampson's children, correspondence regarding the publication of the stories, and a poem.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Drafts of stories for "The Good Giant" written by Sampson while in Cote d'Or, France, in May 1924. Handwritten drafts of "The Energetic Old Woman," "The Kidnapped Husband," "The King of the Pigs," and "The Double Thick."

did.unittitle: The Fact Is: Fables for People like Ourselves

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A collection of short fables.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Introductions to The Ambassadors and Daisy Miller.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A translation of Phèdre, a French dramatic tragedy by Jean Racine. Translation dated as finished on May 9, 1930.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Scholarly publications authored by Sampson in various journals. Includes a pamphlet, "A Problem of American Civilization" (1920) and copies of "Nathaniel Hawthorne" from The Reader's Study (1905).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes "A Night Errant," a short story by Sampson and a loose copy of a poem, "To Professor T.F. Crane," in front cover.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Drafts of a column Sampson began in the Cornell Alumni News. Includes a single page of the Cornell Alumni News with a Swinging Bridge article.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Works written by Sampson about Morris Bishop (1893-1973), a Romance studies scholar and author based at Cornell.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Personal journals kept by Sampson during World War I and for a few years after. Topics often include daily updates on the War, his growing family, and social events he attended. Occasional topics include his thoughts on prohibition, women's suffrage, women's and men's fashions, and household finances.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Topics include World War I and European politics, the Manuscript Club, the sinking of the Lucitania and German submarine warfare, anti-German sentiment in America, Woodrow Wilson and Theodore Roosevelt, the lynching of Leo Frank in Georgia, women's suffrage, family geneaology, and the Ancona affair.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Topics include the Cornell Faculty Military Society, Henry James, the Battle of Verdun, the Sussex affair, the purchase of his first motor car, the death of his mentor J.M. Hart, the Irish Easter Rebellion, the birth of his daughter, the 1916 New York City Polio epidemic, evolving fashion trends, the sinking of the HMHS Britannic, and the Battle of Bucharest.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Topics include the creation of the Cornell unit of the American Ambulance Field Service and its management, the Russian Revolution, the Zimmerman telegram, the United States' declaration of war against Germany, Cornell students going to war, Prime Minister Lloyd George and Foreign Secretary Balfour, Hu Shih, the United States draft, Russia's retreat from the war, air raids in London, and Sampson's involvement in the domestic war effort.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Topics include a review of 1918 and the end of World War I, the formation of the League of Nations, reflections on Theodore Roosevelt, smoking culture, facial hair fashions, Henry James, prohibition, the cost of living in 1920, anti-socialist sentiment in the United States, President J.G. Schurman's resignation, Cornell war degrees, the birth of his fourth child, and musings on decorum, modesty, body hair, and changes in the English language. Includes lists of books read from January 1919 to April 1922.

did.unittitle: Series IV. Indiana University Lecture Notebooks

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Labeled with bookpate of Sydney W. Landon.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Lectures about the Merchant of Venice, Richard III, Macbeth, and Much Ado About Nothing.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Lectures about Much Ado About Nothing (contiuned from vol. 2), Julius Caesar, and Hamlet. Includes an envelope in the front cover of lecture notes on The Tempest.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Lecture notes about Hamlet (continued from vol. 3), Lear, Othello, Anthony and Cleopatra, the Winter's Tale, and The Jew of Malta.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Lecture notes about the Jew of Malta (continued from vol. 4), Tamburlaine, Faustus, Edward II, The Duchess of Malfi, The Broken Heart, and Every Man in His Humor. Includes caricature drawings presumably by Sampson of presumably his fellow English professors at Indiana Univeristy. Each caricature is accompanied by a short poem.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Lecture notes about Two Noble Kinsmen, Philaster, and The Maid's Tragedy. Volume ends on page 19.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Various lecture notes. Topics include the development of drama and dramatic strucutre, Shakespeare, and Greek dramas.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Predominantly lectures on the works of Percy Bysshe Shelley but includes lecture notes on other contemporary poets.
